<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 are ideal for a test zone — the suite is completely self-contained and will not introduce dependency side-effects during refactoring.
[observation] [info] Coverage spans four distinct CLI commands (adapter, analyze, import, prune) but the chunked-review and fix commands each have separate satellite zones with their own unit tests — verify that CLI e2e coverage includes or explicitly defers these commands.
[observation] [info] Zero entry points correctly indicates a test-only zone; the '-tests' suffix is not needed here because no production files are present, but the name should consistently reflect the CLI scope to distinguish it from the 'Rex E2E Config' zone (algorithmicId: rex).
[pattern] [warning] chunked-review and prd-fix-command are the two highest-risk CLI commands (dual-fragility governance, active bidirectional cycles with the domain engine) but neither has a corresponding e2e test file in rex-cli-e2e — adding e2e coverage for these commands would surface cycle-induced initialization failures that unit tests cannot detect.
[relationship] [info] rex-cli-e2e imports from none of the satellite zones (chunked-review, prd-fix-command, rex-core-utilities) and produces zero cross-zone edges — this confirms the e2e suite tests the CLI as a black box rather than white-box testing internals, which is the correct pattern but also means internal zone cycles are invisible to e2e assertions.
[anti-pattern] [warning] The fix and chunked-review commands are the highest-governance-risk CLI commands in the rex package (dual-fragility zones, active bidirectional cycles with the domain engine) but neither has an e2e test file. cli-fix.test.ts and cli-chunked-review.test.ts should be added before any structural consolidation to provide black-box regression coverage.
[suggestion] [info] cli-adapter.test.ts tests CLI wiring structure while the other three files test command behavior — separating adapter structural tests from command behavioral tests into distinct files or subdirectories would make failure triage faster and the zone's coverage scope clearer.
[suggestion] [warning] rex-cli-e2e covers approximately 10% of the 30+ CLI commands (analyze, import, prune plus 1 adapter structural test) — the zone's perfect cohesion metrics give no indication of this coverage gap; add an explicit coverage inventory comment or README in the e2e directory listing which commands are intentionally deferred vs unintentionally uncovered.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling confirm this is a fully self-contained test zone with no production source entanglement.
- Four test files cover distinct CLI workflows (adapter, analyze, import, prune) — breadth of coverage suggests the zone was grown deliberately alongside CLI feature development.
- No entry points are exposed, correctly signaling this zone is never imported by other zones — a clean test-only boundary.
- Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 are ideal for a test zone — the suite is completely self-contained and will not introduce dependency side-effects during refactoring.
- Coverage spans four distinct CLI commands (adapter, analyze, import, prune) but the chunked-review and fix commands each have separate satellite zones with their own unit tests — verify that CLI e2e coverage includes or explicitly defers these commands.
- Zero entry points correctly indicates a test-only zone; the '-tests' suffix is not needed here because no production files are present, but the name should consistently reflect the CLI scope to distinguish it from the 'Rex E2E Config' zone (algorithmicId: rex).
- rex-cli-e2e is the only zone in the rex package with zero bidirectional coupling — it stands as the sole healthy-boundary example in a package where every other non-test zone participates in at least one cycle.
- The four e2e test files cover CLI workflows (adapter, analyze, import, prune) but none of the satellite command zones (chunked-review, prd-fix-command) have corresponding e2e entries in this zone — the CLI e2e suite has a coverage gap for the two most governance-sensitive commands.
- chunked-review and prd-fix-command are the two highest-risk CLI commands (dual-fragility governance, active bidirectional cycles with the domain engine) but neither has a corresponding e2e test file in rex-cli-e2e — adding e2e coverage for these commands would surface cycle-induced initialization failures that unit tests cannot detect.
- rex-cli-e2e imports from none of the satellite zones (chunked-review, prd-fix-command, rex-core-utilities) and produces zero cross-zone edges — this confirms the e2e suite tests the CLI as a black box rather than white-box testing internals, which is the correct pattern but also means internal zone cycles are invisible to e2e assertions.
- The four e2e files cover adapter, analyze, import, and prune — 'prune' has e2e coverage but no satellite unit tests, while 'fix' and 'chunked-review' have satellite unit tests but no e2e coverage. The coverage pattern is inverted from what governance risk would predict: the two highest-risk commands have the weakest test tier.
- Adding an e2e test for the fix command before inlining core/fix.ts would create a black-box safety net that verifies the consolidation does not alter CLI behavior — the refactor can then proceed with regression protection that the current suite lacks.
- The fix and chunked-review commands are the highest-governance-risk CLI commands in the rex package (dual-fragility zones, active bidirectional cycles with the domain engine) but neither has an e2e test file. cli-fix.test.ts and cli-chunked-review.test.ts should be added before any structural consolidation to provide black-box regression coverage.
- The rex CLI has 30+ command files in src/cli/commands/ but rex-cli-e2e covers only 3 command-level workflows (analyze, import, prune) plus 1 structural test (adapter) — approximately 10% command-level coverage. The zone's perfect cohesion (1.0) and zero coupling create a false assurance signal: the metrics indicate a well-structured test zone without revealing the breadth gap.
- cli-adapter.test.ts tests the CLI adapter layer (a structural concern — how commands are wired to the CLI framework) while cli-analyze, cli-import, and cli-prune test specific command behavior — the zone conflates two testing concerns with different change drivers: adapter tests break on framework refactors, command tests break on feature logic changes. This conflation makes it harder to triage e2e failures by root cause.
- rex-cli-e2e covers approximately 10% of the 30+ CLI commands (analyze, import, prune plus 1 adapter structural test) — the zone's perfect cohesion metrics give no indication of this coverage gap; add an explicit coverage inventory comment or README in the e2e directory listing which commands are intentionally deferred vs unintentionally uncovered.
- cli-adapter.test.ts tests CLI wiring structure while the other three files test command behavior — separating adapter structural tests from command behavioral tests into distinct files or subdirectories would make failure triage faster and the zone's coverage scope clearer.
- [call graph] 128 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)
